```text

Let $n=7^3\cdot 13\cdot 167$. Let $\varphi(n)$ denote Euler's totient function, let $\sigma(n)$ denote the sum of positive divisors of $n$, and for a prime $p$ let $v_p(m)$ denote the exponent of $p$ in the prime factorization of the nonzero integer $m$.
Consider the congruence
$\varphi(n)\,x\equiv 13\cdot 167\,\sigma(n)\pmod{n}$.
Among all integers $x$ satisfying this congruence such that $v_{13}(x)=v_{167}(x)=1$ and $x/(13\cdot 167)$ is a perfect square, let $x_0$ be the least positive such $x$.
Determine the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ such that $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$.

Solution.
Lemma 1.
Let $p,q,r$ be distinct odd primes and let $k\ge 2$. Set $n=p^kqr$. Assume $p\nmid (q-1)(r-1)$ and $q\nmid (r-1)$. Then $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$.

Proof.
$\varphi(n)=\varphi(p^k)\varphi(q)\varphi(r)=p^{k-1}(p-1)(q-1)(r-1)$.
Since $p\nmid (q-1)(r-1)$ and $p\nmid (p-1)$, we have $v_p(\varphi(n))=k-1$, while $v_p(n)=k$, so the $p$-part of $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)$ is $p^{k-1}$.
For $q$, if $q\mid\varphi(n)$ then $q$ divides $p^{k-1}(p-1)(q-1)(r-1)$. But $q\nmid p^{k-1}$, $0<p-1<q$ so $q\nmid (p-1)$, and $q\nmid (q-1)$, hence $q\mid (r-1)$, contradicting $q\nmid (r-1)$. Thus $q\nmid \varphi(n)$.
For $r$, we have $r\nmid p^{k-1}$ and $0<p-1<r$, $0<q-1<r$, and $r\nmid (r-1)$, so $r\nmid \varphi(n)$.
Therefore no prime factor of $n$ other than $p$ divides $\varphi(n)$, and $\gcd(\varphi(n),n)=p^{k-1}$. $\square$

Lemma 2.
For a prime $p$ and an integer $k\ge 1$, $\sigma(p^k)\equiv 1\pmod p$, hence $p\nmid \sigma(p^k)$.

Proof.
$\sigma(p^k)=1+p+p^2+\cdots+p^k\equiv 1\pmod p$. $\square$

Lemma 3.
Let $p$ be a prime and let $t_0$ be an integer with $0\le t_0<p$. Assume the congruence $y^2\equiv t_0\pmod p$ is solvable. Let $y_0$ be the least positive integer satisfying $y_0^2\equiv t_0\pmod p$.
Then among all integers of the form $t_0+pt$ with $t\ge 0$ that are perfect squares, the smallest is $y_0^2$.

Proof.
If $t_0+pt$ is a perfect square, write $t_0+pt=y^2$. Then $y^2\equiv t_0\pmod p$, so $y$ is a solution of $y^2\equiv t_0\pmod p$. By minimality of $y_0$, we have $y\ge y_0$, hence $y^2\ge y_0^2$. Thus the smallest square in the progression is $y_0^2$. $\square$

Step 1 (multiplicative_functions).
Since $n=7^3\cdot 13\cdot 167$ is a product of coprime prime powers,
$\varphi(n)=\varphi(7^3)\varphi(13)\varphi(167)$ and $\sigma(n)=\sigma(7^3)\sigma(13)\sigma(167)$.

Step 2 (prime_power_closed_form_evaluation).
$\varphi(7^3)=7^2(7-1)=49\cdot 6=294$, $\varphi(13)=12$, $\varphi(167)=166$, hence $\varphi(n)=294\cdot 12\cdot 166=585648$.
Also $\sigma(7^3)=(7^4-1)/(7-1)=(2401-1)/6=400$, $\sigma(13)=1+13=14$, $\sigma(167)=1+167=168$, hence $\sigma(n)=400\cdot 14\cdot 168=940800$.

Step 3 (p_adic_exponent_tracking; Lemma 1).
Compute $d=\gcd(\varphi(n),n)$.
Here $n=7^3\cdot 13\cdot 167$ with $p=7$, $k=3$, $q=13$, $r=167$. We have $7\nmid (13-1)(167-1)=12\cdot 166$ and $13\nmid (167-1)=166$.
By Lemma 1, $d=7^{3-1}=49$.

Step 4 (p_adic_exponent_tracking; Lemma 2; linear_congruence_solvability).
A congruence $ax\equiv b\pmod n$ has a solution if and only if $\gcd(a,n)\mid b$.
We must check $49\mid (13\cdot 167)\sigma(n)$.
By Lemma 2, $7\nmid \sigma(7^3)=400$, so all $7$-adic valuation in $\sigma(n)=\sigma(7^3)(13+1)(167+1)$ comes from $(13+1)(167+1)=14\cdot 168$.
Since $v_7(14)=1$ and $v_7(168)=1$, we get $v_7(\sigma(n))=2$, hence $49\mid \sigma(n)$ and therefore $49\mid (13\cdot 167)\sigma(n)$.
So solutions exist.

Step 5 (linear_congruence_gcd_reduction_and_inverse).
Divide the congruence and modulus by $d=49$:
$(\varphi(n)/49)x\equiv (13\cdot 167)(\sigma(n)/49)\pmod{n/49}$.
Compute $n/49=7\cdot 13\cdot 167=15197$, $\varphi(n)/49=585648/49=11952$, and $\sigma(n)/49=940800/49=19200$.
Thus the reduced congruence is
$11952x\equiv (13\cdot 167)\cdot 19200\pmod{15197}$.

Step 6 (invertibility check).
$15197=7\cdot 13\cdot 167$, while $11952=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 83$, so $\gcd(11952,15197)=1$.
Hence the reduced congruence has a unique solution class modulo $15197$.

Step 7 (Chinese_remainder_theorem, modulo $13$).
Reduce Step 5 modulo $13$. The right-hand side has a factor $13$, so it is $0$ modulo $13$.
Since $\gcd(11952,13)=1$, cancellation gives $x\equiv 0\pmod{13}$.

Step 8 (Chinese_remainder_theorem, modulo $167$).
Reduce Step 5 modulo $167$. The right-hand side has a factor $167$, so it is $0$ modulo $167$.
Since $\gcd(11952,167)=1$, cancellation gives $x\equiv 0\pmod{167}$.

Step 9 (Chinese_remainder_theorem, modulo $7$).
Reduce Step 5 modulo $7$.
$11952\equiv 3\pmod 7$ since $11952-7\cdot 1707=3$.
Also $13\cdot 167\equiv 6\cdot 6\equiv 1\pmod 7$, and $19200\equiv 6\pmod 7$ since $19200-7\cdot 2742=6$.
Thus $3x\equiv 6\pmod 7$, so $x\equiv 2\pmod 7$.

Step 10 (CRT recombination and lifting).
From Steps 7 and 8, $x\equiv 0\pmod{13\cdot 167}$, so write $x=2171t$.
Step 9 gives $2171t\equiv 2\pmod 7$. Since $2171\equiv 1\pmod 7$, this is $t\equiv 2\pmod 7$, so $t=2+7s$ for some integer $s$.
Therefore every solution of the reduced congruence has the form $x=2171(2+7s)$, equivalently $x=4342+15197s$.
Because the reduced congruence class is unique modulo $15197$, the full set of integer solutions to the original congruence is
$x=4342+15197s$ for $s\in\mathbb{Z}$.

Step 11 (quadratic_residue_via_Euler_criterion; Lemma 3).
Impose the square condition. From Step 10,
$x/(13\cdot 167)=x/2171=2+7s$.
Thus the condition that $x/(13\cdot 167)$ is a perfect square is
$2+7s=y^2$ for some integer $y\ge 1$.
This requires $y^2\equiv 2\pmod 7$. By Euler's criterion,
$2^{(7-1)/2}=2^3=8\equiv 1\pmod 7$,
so $2$ is a quadratic residue modulo $7$, and solutions exist. In fact $3^2\equiv 2\pmod 7$.
Let $y_0$ be the least positive solution of $y^2\equiv 2\pmod 7$. Checking $y=1,2,3$ gives $1^2\equiv 1$, $2^2\equiv 4$, $3^2\equiv 2\pmod 7$, so $y_0=3$.
By Lemma 3, the smallest perfect square among integers of the form $2+7s$ with $s\ge 0$ is $y_0^2=9$, corresponding to $s=(9-2)/7=1$.

Step 12 (valuation filter).
With $s=1$, Step 10 gives $x_0=2171\cdot 9=19539$.
Since $19539=13\cdot 167\cdot 9$ and $\gcd(9,13\cdot 167)=1$, we have $v_{13}(x_0)=v_{167}(x_0)=1$.
Also $s=0$ gives $x=4342$ but $x/2171=2$ is not a square, and any admissible square has $y\ge y_0=3$ so $x\ge 2171\cdot 9$, hence this $x_0$ is the least positive solution satisfying all conditions.

Step 13 (modular_normalization_mod_999).
Compute $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$ with $0\le N\le 998$.
$19539=999\cdot 19+558$, so $N=558$.

Answer: $558$.

3) Uniqueness Test
Let $A=\varphi(n)$, $B=(13\cdot 167)\sigma(n)$, and $n=7^3\cdot 13\cdot 167$.
By Lemma 1, $\gcd(A,n)=49$. By Step 4, $49\mid B$, so the congruence $Ax\equiv B\pmod n$ is solvable and reduces to a congruence modulo $n/49=15197$.
Step 6 shows $\gcd(A/49,15197)=1$, hence the reduced congruence has exactly one residue class modulo $15197$; thus all integer solutions to the original congruence form a single arithmetic progression $x=x_*+15197s$.

Solving the reduced congruence modulo $13$, $167$, and $7$ forces $x_*\equiv 0\pmod{13}$, $x_*\equiv 0\pmod{167}$, and $x_*\equiv 2\pmod 7$, hence every solution has the form $x=2171(2+7s)$.
The square filter forces $2+7s=y^2$ with $s\ge 0$. The smallest positive $y$ with $y^2\equiv 2\pmod 7$ is $y_0=3$, so the smallest admissible square is $y_0^2=9$, giving the unique least positive admissible solution $x_0=2171\cdot 9=19539$.
Then $N\equiv x_0\pmod{999}$ with $0\le N\le 998$ is uniquely $N=558$.

558
